_note: Server-owned V3 curriculum. Only authenticated callables return it; submitStage uses this same file for stage existence and ordering. No copy is deployed in the Amplify public assets.

fasttrack.title: 4-Week Fast Track — Prompt Like a Pro

stages.label: Day 1
stages.title: Token Mechanics
stages.deliverable: Read the OpenAI tokenizer docs + tiktoken library. Exercise: count tokens in 5 prompts you wrote before — see the waste.

stages.label: Day 2
stages.title: LLM Parameters
stages.deliverable: Study temperature, top_p, max_tokens, stop sequences. Exercise: run the same prompt at temp=0 vs temp=1 — document the difference.

stages.label: Day 3
stages.title: Context Windows
stages.deliverable: Study context-window limits (GPT-4o 128K, Claude 3.5 200K, Gemini 1.5 1M). Learn what happens when context overflows — silent truncation is dangerous.

stages.label: Day 4
stages.title: Model Selection
stages.deliverable: Study when to use GPT-4o-mini vs GPT-4o vs o3 vs Claude Haiku vs Sonnet. Key insight: do NOT use the most powerful model for simple tasks.

stages.label: Day 5
stages.title: Prompt Basics
stages.deliverable: Study zero-shot, one-shot, few-shot prompting — when each applies. Learn system vs user vs assistant prefill prompts.

stages.label: Day 6
stages.title: Environmental Audit
stages.deliverable: Read 'The Real Carbon Cost of an AI Token'. Calculate: estimate the CO2 cost of your last week of AI usage.

stages.label: Day 7
stages.title: Week 1 Project
stages.deliverable: Build a Python token counter that analyzes any prompt and reports token count, estimated cost at GPT-4o rates, estimated CO2 equivalent, and a wasteful-pattern flag.

stages.label: Day 8
stages.title: SCALE Framework
stages.deliverable: Study and memorize the SCALE framework. Exercise: take 5 prompts you wrote before and re-write each using SCALE.

stages.label: Day 9
stages.title: Token Optimization
stages.deliverable: Apply all 10 optimization techniques. Exercise: compress a bloated 50+ token prompt to under 20 tokens without losing output quality.

stages.label: Day 10
stages.title: Idempotency & Fault Tolerance
stages.deliverable: Study idempotency in distributed systems (Martin Fowler). Read 'Designing for failure' — AWS Well-Architected Framework.

stages.label: Day 11
stages.title: Scale Prompting
stages.deliverable: Prompt for code that processes a 1MB file, then extend the same prompt for 1TB. Document what changes and what new constraints appear.

stages.label: Day 12
stages.title: Model Selection Lab
stages.deliverable: Run the same coding task on GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o, Claude Haiku, Claude Sonnet. Compare output quality, token count, cost, and latency.

stages.label: Day 13
stages.title: Anti-Pattern Audit
stages.deliverable: Review 10 pieces of code you or AI generated. Identify anti-patterns: no error handling? no scale constraints? no idempotency?

stages.label: Day 14
stages.title: Week 2 Capstone
stages.deliverable: Build a prompt-engineering audit tool: a Python script that flags missing scale requirements, missing error handling, missing output-format spec, and estimated token waste.

stages.label: Day 15
stages.title: Architecture Design
stages.deliverable: Apply the SCALE framework to design your project — document all 5 questions. Draw an architecture diagram (Excalidraw).

stages.label: Day 16
stages.title: Core Build
stages.deliverable: Use SCALE-optimized prompts exclusively. Build the core data-processing logic with AI assistance.

stages.label: Day 17
stages.title: Idempotency + Scale
stages.deliverable: Implement idempotency (checkpoint files or DB state tracking). Test: run the pipeline twice on the same input; verify no duplicate processing.

stages.label: Day 18
stages.title: Error Handling + Logging
stages.deliverable: Add structured logging (structlog or loguru). Implement retry logic with exponential backoff.

stages.label: Day 19
stages.title: Token Audit
stages.deliverable: Run your AI session logs through the Week-1 token counter. Document how many tokens the project used and at what cost.

stages.label: Day 20
stages.title: Deploy
stages.deliverable: Deploy to Railway or Render (free tier). Set up GitHub Actions for automated testing on every commit (CI/CD).

stages.label: Day 21
stages.title: Share
stages.deliverable: Post on LinkedIn: 'Week 3 of the Code For Good fast track. I built [project]. Here is what I learned about prompting for production code.'

stages.label: Day 22
stages.title: LinkedIn Profile
stages.deliverable: Rewrite your headline: not 'CS Student' but 'AI-Augmented Developer | Enterprise Prompt Engineering | Distributed Systems'.

stages.label: Day 23
stages.title: Certification Fast-Track
stages.deliverable: Pick ONE: AWS Cloud Practitioner (exam $100, 1-2 weeks) OR Google Data Analytics Certificate (4-6 weeks) OR Databricks Associate (free voucher).

stages.label: Day 24
stages.title: Gig Economy Setup
stages.deliverable: Create Upwork and Contra profiles. Niche: 'I build token-optimized AI pipelines and prompt-engineering workflows for businesses.'

stages.label: Day 25
stages.title: Interview Prep — Technical
stages.deliverable: Study the 5 prompt-engineering questions hiring managers now ask. Practice answering 'How would you optimize this prompt for production?' without notes.

stages.label: Day 26
stages.title: Direct Outreach
stages.deliverable: Identify 10 startups building AI products (Product Hunt, Wellfound, YC list). Send 10 cold LinkedIn messages using the outreach template.

stages.label: Day 27
stages.title: Freelance First Proposal
stages.deliverable: Write your first Upwork proposal for a relevant gig. Apply SCALE thinking to the proposal itself — their scale, constraints, and output needs.

stages.label: Day 28
stages.title: Program Complete
stages.deliverable: Record a 5-minute Loom: 'What I built and learned in 4 weeks at Code For Good.' Post a full LinkedIn reflection on what you can now do.

stages.label: Pillar 1
stages.title: Build AI-Augmented Skills
stages.description: The most in-demand STEM graduates aren't those who know the most code — they're the ones who work with AI tools to produce 10x the output. Every employer now expects new hires to use AI productively from day one.
milestones: Install and use GitHub Copilot (free for students via the GitHub Education Pack)
Learn the SCALE framework and apply it to every prompt you write
Learn LLM basics: tokens, context windows, temperature, system prompts
Set up Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini — know each one's strengths
Build one small automation using an AI API (OpenAI, Anthropic, or Gemini)

stages.label: Pillar 2
stages.title: Build a Portfolio of Real Projects
stages.description: Your GPA tells employers what you were taught; your portfolio tells them what you can actually do. A deployed project is the proof that stands out.
milestones: Deployed: live at a URL (Vercel, Railway, Render, or HuggingFace Spaces — all free tiers)
Documented: a clear README — problem, approach, tech stack, token decisions, how to run
Functional and original: it works, and it solves a real problem (not a tutorial clone)
Measurable: include a metric — cost per query, response time, accuracy, or active users
Polish your GitHub profile: photo, bio, pinned repos, profile README

stages.label: Pillar 3
stages.title: Enter the Gig Economy
stages.description: Freelancing is proof of real client work, business communication, and a track record no internship can replicate. You can start in Week 3.
milestones: Create profiles on Upwork, Fiverr, and Contra
Write a specific bio: 'I build Python automation scripts and AI chatbots for small businesses'
Set an initial low rate to build reviews; complete your profile 100%
On Fiverr, create 3 specific gigs; on Upwork, apply to 5 jobs/day with custom proposals
Offer a small automation/chatbot to 5 local small businesses for $50-100

stages.label: Pillar 4
stages.title: Build a Personal Brand & Network
stages.description: In a sea of identical degrees, a visible online presence is what makes recruiters reach out to you.
milestones: Upgrade LinkedIn: headshot, clear headline, a story-driven about section
Add all projects, skills, and experience; connect with 50 people you know
Follow 20 thought leaders and engage thoughtfully
Post your first article: 'What I learned building my first AI app'
Commit to posting weekly: Problem → How you solved it → Lesson learned

stages.label: Pillar 5
stages.title: Target the Hidden Job Market
stages.description: 70-80% of jobs are never publicly posted. Micro-internships are short paid sprints that open those doors.
milestones: Sign up for Parker Dewey and complete your profile
Complete 2-3 Forage virtual experience programs (used to pre-screen candidates)
Browse Wellfound for early-stage startup roles
Make a list of 20 startups; research 5 deeply
Send cold outreach to 10 startup founders / engineering leads per week

stages.label: Pillar 6
stages.title: Stack Certifications Strategically
stages.description: The right certs, strategically chosen, are a powerful signal of competence when you lack years of experience.
milestones: Dev: AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner (1-2 months, $100)
Dev: Meta Front-End or Back-End Developer Certificate (Coursera)
Data: Google Data Analytics Certificate (Coursera, ~6 months)
Data: Databricks Certified Data Analyst Associate (free voucher often available)
Bridge: Google Project Management Certificate (for lead/product roles)

stages.label: Pillar 7
stages.title: Master the Tools Industry Uses
stages.description: Universities teach concepts; industry hires people who know specific tools. This pillar closes that gap.
milestones: Go beyond git add/commit/push: branching, merging, rebasing, cherry-pick
Learn GitFlow and practice pull requests + code review on GitHub
Master SQL beyond SELECT: JOINs, GROUP BY, window functions, CTEs
Learn one BI tool: Power BI (free desktop) or Tableau Public
Connect SQL to Python with pandas + SQLAlchemy

stages.label: Pillar 8
stages.title: Solve Real Community Problems
stages.description: The most overlooked career accelerator is also the most fulfilling: using your skills to solve real problems for communities you're part of.
milestones: List communities you're part of; for each, find a slow/manual process to automate
Pick ONE community and ONE specific problem to solve first
Meet the community leader for 30 minutes to understand the exact pain point
Build the simplest version that works; deploy it to at least 10 active users
Write a case study with numbers (e.g. 'cut scheduling from 3 hours to 20 minutes')
